The use of [slave](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") and [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour") in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Zwangsarbeit*) and throughout [German-occupied Europe](/wiki/German-occupied_Europe "German-occupied Europe") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") took place on an unprecedented scale.[[2]](#cite_note-Herbert-2) It was a vital part of the [German economic exploitation](/wiki/Economics_of_fascism#Political_economy_of_Nazi_Germany "Economics of fascism") of conquered territories. It also contributed to the mass extermination of populations in occupied Europe. The Germans abducted approximately 12 million people from almost twenty European countries; about two thirds came from [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe").[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1)

Many workers died as a result of their living conditions – extreme mistreatment, severe [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition") and abuse were the main causes of death. Many more became [civilian casualties](/wiki/Civilian_casualties "Civilian casualties") from enemy (Allied) bombing and shelling of their workplaces throughout the war.[[3]](#cite_note-Łuczak-3) At the peak of the program, the forced labourers constituted 20% of the German work force. Counting deaths and turnover, about 15 million men and women were forced labourers at one point during the war.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Besides Jews, the harshest deportation and forced labor policies were applied to the populations of Belarus, Ukraine, and Russia. By the end of the war, half of Belarus's population had been either killed or deported.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The defeat of Nazi Germany in 1945 freed approximately 11 million foreigners (categorized as "displaced persons"), most of whom were forced labourers and POWs. During the war, German forces brought into the Reich 6.5 million civilians, in addition to Soviet POWs, for [unfree labour](/wiki/Unfree_labour "Unfree labour") in factories.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1) Returning them home was a high priority for the Allies. However returning citizens of the USSR were often meant suspicion of collaboration or reincarceration in a [Gulag](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag") prison camp. The [United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration](/wiki/United_Nations_Relief_and_Rehabilitation_Administration "United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration") (UNRRA), [Red Cross](/wiki/International_Red_Cross_and_Red_Crescent_Movement "International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement"), and military operations provided food, clothing, shelter, and assistance in returning home. In all, 5.2 million foreign workers and POWs were repatriated to the Soviet Union, 1.6 million to Poland, 1.5 million to France, and 900,000 to Italy, along with 300,000 to 400,000 each to Yugoslavia, Czechoslovakia, the Netherlands, Hungary, and Belgium.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Forced workers

See also: [Service du travail obligatoire](/wiki/Service_du_travail_obligatoire "Service du travail obligatoire") and [Arbeitsamt in occupied Poland](/wiki/Arbeitsamt_in_occupied_Poland "Arbeitsamt in occupied Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Chod%C5%BAmy_na_roboty_rolne_do_Niemiec.jpg/500px-Chod%C5%BAmy_na_roboty_rolne_do_Niemiec.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chod%C5%BAmy_na_roboty_rolne_do_Niemiec.jpg)

German [Polish-language](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") recruitment poster: "'Let's do farm work in Germany!' See your *[wójt](/wiki/W%C3%B3jt "Wójt")* at once."

[Hitler](/wiki/Hitler "Hitler")'s policy of *[Lebensraum](/wiki/Lebensraum "Lebensraum")* ('room for living') strongly emphasized conquest of lands in the East, known as *[Generalplan Ost](/wiki/Generalplan_Ost "Generalplan Ost")*, and the exploitation of these lands to provide cheap goods and labour for Germany. Even before the war, [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") maintained a supply of [slave labour](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery"). This practice started in the early days of [labour camps](/wiki/Labour_camps "Labour camps") for "unreliable elements" ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *unzuverlässige Elemente*), such as
[homosexuals](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality"), criminals, political [dissidents](/wiki/Dissident "Dissident"), [communists](/wiki/Communism "Communism"), [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew"), the homeless and anyone the regime wanted out of the way. During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the [Nazis](/wiki/Nazis "Nazis") operated several categories of *[Arbeitslager](/wiki/Arbeitslager "Arbeitslager")* (labour camps) for different categories of inmates. Prisoners in Nazi labour camps were worked to death on short rations in lethal conditions, or killed if they became unable to work. Many died as a direct result of forced labour under the Nazis.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1)

After the [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland"), [Polish Jews](/wiki/Polish_Jews "Polish Jews") over the age of 12 and Poles over the age of 12 living in the [General Government](/wiki/General_Government "General Government") territory were subject to forced labor.[[8]](#cite_note-Majer2003-8) Historian Jan Gross estimates that "no more than 15 percent" of Polish workers volunteered to go to work in Germany.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1942, all non-Germans living in the General Government were subject to forced labor.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The largest number of labour camps held civilians forcibly abducted in the occupied countries (see [Łapanka](/wiki/%C5%81apanka "Łapanka")) to provide labour in the German war industry, repair bombed railroads and bridges, or work on farms. [Manual labour](/wiki/Manual_labour "Manual labour") was in high demand, as much of the work that today would be [done with machines](/wiki/Mechanization "Mechanization") was still done by hand in the 1930s and 1940s, such as digging, [material handling](/wiki/Material_handling "Material handling"), or [machining](/wiki/Machining "Machining"). As the war progressed, the use of slave labour increased massively. [Prisoners of war](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war "Prisoner of war") and civilian "undesirables" were brought in from occupied territories. Millions of Jews, [Slavs](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples") and other conquered peoples were used as slave labourers by German corporations including [Thyssen](/wiki/Fritz_Thyssen "Fritz Thyssen"), [Krupp](/wiki/Krupp "Krupp"), [IG Farben](/wiki/IG_Farben "IG Farben"), [Bosch](/wiki/Robert_Bosch_GmbH "Robert Bosch GmbH"), [Daimler-Benz](/wiki/Daimler-Benz "Daimler-Benz"), [Demag](/wiki/Demag "Demag"), [Henschel](/wiki/Henschel "Henschel"), [Junkers](/wiki/Junkers "Junkers"), [Messerschmitt](/wiki/Messerschmitt "Messerschmitt"), [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens"), and [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen"),[[11]](#cite_note-Buggeln-11) not to mention the German subsidiaries of foreign firms, such as *[Fordwerke](/wiki/Fordwerke "Fordwerke")* (a subsidiary of [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company")) and [Adam Opel AG](/wiki/Opel "Opel") (a subsidiary of [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors")) among others.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Once the war had begun, the foreign subsidiaries were seized and [nationalized](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") by the Nazi-controlled German state, and work conditions deteriorated, as they did throughout German industry. About 12 million forced labourers, most of whom were [Eastern Europeans](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), were employed in the [German war economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Nazi_Germany "Economy of Nazi Germany") inside [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") during the war.[[13]](#cite_note-MMDW-13) The German need for slave labour grew to the point that even children were kidnapped as labor, in an operation called the *[Heu-Aktion](/wiki/Heu-Aktion "Heu-Aktion")*.

More than 2,000 German companies profited from slave labour during the Nazi era, including [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank "Deutsche Bank") and [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Classifications

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Arbeitsbuch_Fur_Auslander_1942.jpg/330px-Arbeitsbuch_Fur_Auslander_1942.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arbeitsbuch_Fur_Auslander_1942.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-78612-0004%2C_KZ_Sachsenhausen%2C_H%C3%A4ftlinge_bei_Erdarbeiten.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-78612-0004%2C_KZ_Sachsenhausen%2C_H%C3%A4ftlinge_bei_Erdarbeiten.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-78612-0004,_KZ_Sachsenhausen,_H%C3%A4ftlinge_bei_Erdarbeiten.jpg)

A class system was created amongst *Fremdarbeiter* ('foreign workers') brought to Germany to work for the Reich. The system was based on layers of increasingly less privileged workers, starting with well-paid workers from German allies or neutral countries to forced labourers from conquered *[Untermenschen](/wiki/Untermensch "Untermensch")* ('sub-humans') populations.

* ***Gastarbeitnehmer*** ('[guest workers](/wiki/Guest_workers "Guest workers")') – Workers from Germanic and Scandinavian countries, France, Italy,[[15]](#cite_note-UH-15) other German allies (Romania, Bulgaria, Hungary), and friendly neutrals (e.g. Spain and Switzerland). Only about 1% of foreign workers in Germany came from countries that were neutral or allied to Germany.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1)
* ***Zwangsarbeiter*** (forced workers) – Forced labourers from countries not allied with Germany. This class of workers was broken down into the following designations:
  + *[Militärinternierte](/wiki/Internment "Internment")* ('military internees') – Prisoners of war. Geneva Conventions allowed captor nations to force non-officer prisoners of war to work, within certain restrictions. For example, almost all Polish non-officer prisoners of war (c. 300,000) were forced to work in Nazi Germany. In 1944, almost 2 million prisoners of war worked as forced labourers in Germany.[[15]](#cite_note-UH-15) Compared to other foreign workers, prisoners of war were relatively well-off, especially if they came from Western countries that were still at war, such as the United States or Great Britain, as the minimum standards of their treatment were mandated by the [Geneva Conventions](/wiki/Geneva_Conventions "Geneva Conventions"). Their working conditions and well-being were subject to supervision by the International Red Cross and, in cases of mistreatment, retaliation against German prisoners held in the US, Britain and Canada (who performed similar forced labor) was almost certain. However, the treatment of these workers varied greatly depending on their country of origin, the period, and the specific workplace. In particular, Soviet prisoners of war were treated with utter brutality, as Nazis did not consider them protected under the Geneva Conventions, which had not been ratified or implemented by the Soviet Union.
  + *[Zivilarbeiter](/wiki/Zivilarbeiter "Zivilarbeiter")* ('civilian workers') – ethnic Poles from the [General Government](/wiki/General_Government "General Government") territory.[[15]](#cite_note-UH-15) They were regulated by strict *[Polish decrees](/wiki/Polish_decrees "Polish decrees")*: they received much lower wages and could not use conveniences such as public transport, or visit many public spaces or businesses. For example, they could not visit German church services, swimming pools, or restaurants; they had to work longer hours and were assigned smaller food rations, and they were subject to a [curfew](/wiki/Curfew "Curfew"). Poles were routinely denied holidays and had to work seven days a week. They could not marry without a permit; they could not possess money or objects of value, even bicycles, cameras, or [lighters](/wiki/Lighter "Lighter"). They were required to wear a badge: the "Polish P", on their clothing. In 1939, there were about 300,000 Polish *Zivilarbeiter* in Germany.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1)[[15]](#cite_note-UH-15) By 1944, their number had skyrocketed to about 1.7 million,[[15]](#cite_note-UH-15) or 2.8 million by different accounts (approximately 10% of occupied Poland's prisoner workforce).[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 1944, there were about 7.6 million foreign so-called 'civilian workers' employed in Germany in total, including POWs from *Generalgouvernement* and the expanded USSR,[[15]](#cite_note-UH-15) with a similar number of workers in this category from other countries.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1)
  + *[Ostarbeiter](/wiki/Ostarbeiter "Ostarbeiter")* ('Eastern workers') – Soviet and Polish civil workers, mostly rounded up in *[Distrikt Galizien](/wiki/Distrikt_Galizien "Distrikt Galizien")* and in *[Reichskommissariat Ukraine](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Ukraine "Reichskommissariat Ukraine")*. They wore an "OST" (East) badge and had to live under guard in camps fenced with barbed wire, and were particularly vulnerable to the whims of the [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo") and the industrial plant guards. Estimates put the number of OST workers between three and 5.5 million.[[17]](#cite_note-HS-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Zvezda2-18)

In general, foreign labourers from Western Europe had similar gross earnings and were subject to similar taxation as German workers. In contrast, Central and Eastern European forced labourers received at most about one-half the gross earnings paid to German workers and had far fewer social benefits.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1) Prisoners of labour or concentration camps received little if any wages or benefits.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1) The deficiency in net earnings of Central and Eastern European forced labourers (versus forced labourers from Western countries) is illustrated by the wage savings forced labourers were able to transfer to their families at home or abroad (see table).

### Sexual slavery

The Nazis issued a [ban on sexual relations between Germans and foreign workers](/wiki/Rassenschande "Rassenschande").[[19]](#cite_note-19) Repeated efforts were made to propagate *Volkstum* ('racial consciousness'), to prevent such relations.[[20]](#cite_note-hertzstein-20): 139 Pamphlets, for instance, instructed all German women to avoid physical contact with any foreign workers brought to Germany as a danger to their blood.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Women who disobeyed were imprisoned[[20]](#cite_note-hertzstein-20): 212 although executions also took place.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Even fraternization with the workers was regarded as dangerous, and targeted by pamphlet campaigns in 1940–1942.[[20]](#cite_note-hertzstein-20): 211–2 Soldiers in the *[Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht")* and [SS](/wiki/SS "SS") officers were exempt from any such restrictions. It is estimated that at least 34,140 Eastern European women apprehended in [Łapankas](/wiki/%C5%81apanka "Łapanka") (military kidnapping raids) were forced to serve them as sex slaves in [German military brothels](/wiki/German_military_brothels_in_World_War_II "German military brothels in World War II") and [camp brothels](/wiki/German_camp_brothels_in_World_War_II "German camp brothels in World War II") during the Third Reich.[[23]](#cite_note-Herbermann-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Lenten-24) In [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") alone, five such establishments were set up under military guard in September 1942, with over 20 rooms each. Alcohol was not allowed, unlike on the Western front, and the victims underwent genital checkups once a week.[[25]](#cite_note-polityka-25)

### French shipyards

French workers at naval bases provided the *[Kriegsmarine](/wiki/Kriegsmarine "Kriegsmarine")* with an essential workforce, thereby supporting Nazi Germany in the [Battle of the Atlantic](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Atlantic "Battle of the Atlantic"). By 1939, the *Kriegsmarine*'s planning had presumed that they had time to build up resources before the war started. When France fell and the ports of [Brest](/wiki/Brest,_France "Brest, France"), [Lorient](/wiki/Lorient "Lorient") and [Saint-Nazaire](/wiki/Saint-Nazaire "Saint-Nazaire") became available, there were insufficient Germans to man these repair and maintenance facilities, so huge reliance was made on the French workforce. At the end of 1940, the *Kriegsmarine* requested 2,700 skilled workers from [Wilhelmshaven](/wiki/Wilhelmshaven "Wilhelmshaven") to work in bases on the Atlantic coast, but this was out of a total available workforce of only 3,300. This same request included 870 men skilled in machinery and engine building, but there were only 725 people with these skills in Wilhelmshaven. This massive deficit was made up of French naval dockyard workers. In February 1941, the naval dockyard at Brest had only 470 German workers, compared with 6,349 French workers. In April 1941, French workers replaced defective [superheater](/wiki/Superheater "Superheater") tubes on the *[Scharnhorst](/wiki/German_battleship_Scharnhorst "German battleship Scharnhorst")*, carrying out the work slowly but, in the opinion of Scharnhorst's captain, to a better standard than could be obtained in the yards in Germany. An assessment commissioned by Vizeadmiral Walter Matthiae in October 1942 of the potential effect of withdrawal of French dockyard workers (considered possible after 32 French fatalities in an air raid at [Lorient Submarine Base](/wiki/Lorient_Submarine_Base "Lorient Submarine Base")) stated that all repairs on the surface fleet would cease and U-boat repairs would be cut by 30 per cent. Admiral [François Darlan](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Darlan "François Darlan") stated on 30 September 1940 that it was useless to decline German requests for collaboration. In September 1942, Rear Admiral Germain Paul Jardel, commander of the French navy in the occupied zone, stated "We have a special interest in that the workers at our arsenals work, and that they work in the arsenals and not in Germany." From a practical point of view, French workers needed employment and could have been conscripted to work in Germany (as happened to 1 million of them).[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1) A small number objected to carrying out war work but the majority were found by the Germans to be willing and efficient workers.[[26]](#cite_note-Hellwinkel-26)

### Numbers

In the late summer of 1944, German records listed 7.6 million foreign civilian workers and [prisoners of war](/wiki/Prisoners_of_war "Prisoners of war") in the German territory, most of whom had been brought there by coercion.[[15]](#cite_note-UH-15) By 1944, slave labour made up one quarter of Germany's entire work force, and the majority of German factories had a contingent of prisoners.[[15]](#cite_note-UH-15)[[27]](#cite_note-27) The Nazis also had plans for the internment and transportation to Europe of "the able-bodied male population between the ages of seventeen and forty-five" in the event of a successful [invasion of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Operation_Sealion "Operation Sealion").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Organisation Todt

Main article: [Organisation Todt](/wiki/Organisation_Todt "Organisation Todt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Female_foreign_workers_from_Stadelheim_prison_work_in_a_factory_owned_by_the_AGFA_camera_company.jpg/330px-Female_foreign_workers_from_Stadelheim_prison_work_in_a_factory_owned_by_the_AGFA_camera_company.jpg)](/wiki/File:Female_foreign_workers_from_Stadelheim_prison_work_in_a_factory_owned_by_the_AGFA_camera_company.jpg)

Foreign workers from [Stadelheim Prison](/wiki/Stadelheim_Prison "Stadelheim Prison") work in a factory owned by the [AGFA](/wiki/AGFA "AGFA") camera company.

[Organisation Todt](/wiki/Organisation_Todt "Organisation Todt") was a [Nazi era](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") [civil](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering") and [military engineering](/wiki/Military_engineer "Military engineer") group in Nazi Germany, eponymously named for its founder [Fritz Todt](/wiki/Fritz_Todt "Fritz Todt"), an engineer and senior [Nazi](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") figure. The organization was responsible for a huge range of engineering projects both in pre-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), and in [occupied Europe](/wiki/German-occupied_Europe "German-occupied Europe") from France to Russia. Todt became notorious for using [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour"). Most so-called "volunteer" Soviet POW workers were assigned to the Organisation Todt.[[29]](#cite_note-30) The history of the organization falls into three main phases:[[30]](#cite_note-HBC-31)

1. A pre-war period between 1933 and 1938, during which the predecessor of Organisation Todt, the office of General Inspector of German Roadways (*Generalinspektor für das deutsche Straßenwesen*), was primarily responsible for the construction of the German *[Autobahn](/wiki/Autobahn "Autobahn")* network. The organisation was able to draw on "conscripted" (i.e. compulsory) labour from within Germany through the Reich Labour Service (*[Reichsarbeitsdienst](/wiki/Reichsarbeitsdienst "Reichsarbeitsdienst")*, RAD).
2. The period from 1938 to 1942, after [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa"), when the Organisation Todt proper was founded and used on the Eastern front. The huge increase in the demand for labour created by the various military and paramilitary projects was met by a series of expansions of the laws on compulsory service, which ultimately obligated all Germans to arbitrarily determined (i.e. effectively unlimited) compulsory labour for the state: *Zwangsarbeit*.[[31]](#cite_note-32) From 1938 to 1940, over 1.75 million Germans were conscripted into labour service. In 1940–42, Organization Todt began to rely on *Gastarbeitnehmer* ([guest workers](/wiki/Guest_workers "Guest workers")), *Militärinternierte* ([military internees](/wiki/Internment "Internment")), *Zivilarbeiter* ([civilian workers](/wiki/Zivilarbeiter "Zivilarbeiter")), *Ostarbeiter* ([Eastern workers](/wiki/Ostarbeiter "Ostarbeiter")) and *Hilfswillige* ("volunteer") POW workers.
3. The period from 1942 until the end of the war, with approximately 1.4 million labourers in the service of the Organisation Todt. Overall, 1% were Germans rejected for military service and 1.5% were concentration camp prisoners the rest were prisoners of war and compulsory labourers from occupied countries. All were effectively treated as slaves and existed in the complete and arbitrary service of a ruthless totalitarian state. Many did not survive the work or the war.[[30]](#cite_note-HBC-31)

Millions of Jews were forced labourers in [ghettos](/wiki/Ghetto "Ghetto"), before they were shipped off to [extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp"). The Nazis also operated [concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps"), some of which provided free forced labour for industrial and other jobs while others existed solely to [exterminate their inmates](/wiki/Nazi_extermination_camp "Nazi extermination camp"). To mislead the victims, at the entrances to a number of camps the lie 'work brings freedom' (*[arbeit macht frei](/wiki/Arbeit_macht_frei "Arbeit macht frei")*) was placed, to encourage the false impression that cooperation would earn release. A notable example of a labour-concentration camp is [Mittelbau-Dora](/wiki/Mittelbau-Dora "Mittelbau-Dora"), a labour camp complex that produced [V-2 rockets](/wiki/V-2_rocket "V-2 rocket"). [Extermination through labour](/wiki/Extermination_through_labour "Extermination through labour") was a Nazi German principle that regulated most of their [labour](/wiki/Labour_camp "Labour camp") and concentration camps.[[32]](#cite_note-Dobosiewicz-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) The rule demanded that inmates of German World War II camps be forced to work for the German war industry with only basic tools and minimal food rations until totally exhausted.[[32]](#cite_note-Dobosiewicz-33)[[34]](#cite_note-35)

## Controversy over compensation

Further information: [World War II reparations](/wiki/World_War_II_reparations "World War II reparations")

To benefit the economy after the war, certain categories of victims of Nazism were excluded from compensation by the German government; these groups had the least political influence they could have brought to bear, and many forced labourers from Eastern Europe fall into this category.[[35]](#cite_note-Dingell-36) There has been little effort by businesses or the German government to compensate forced labourers from the war period.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1)

As stated in the [London Debt Agreement of 1953](/wiki/London_Debt_Agreement_of_1953 "London Debt Agreement of 1953"):

> Consideration of claims arising out of the Second World War by countries which were at war with or were occupied by Germany during that war, and by nationals of such countries, against the Reich and agencies of the Reich, including costs of German occupation, credits acquired during occupation on clearing accounts and claims against the *Reichskreditkassen* shall be deferred until the final settlement of the problem of reparations.

To this day,[*[as of?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] there are arguments that such settlement has never been fully carried out. German post-war development has been greatly aided, while the development of victim countries has stalled.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1)

A prominent example of a group which received almost no compensation is the Polish forced labourers. According to the [Potsdam Agreements](/wiki/Potsdam_Agreements "Potsdam Agreements") of 1945, the Poles were to receive [reparations](/wiki/War_reparations "War reparations") not from Germany itself, but from the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")'s share of those reparations; under Soviet pressure on the Polish Communist government, the Poles agreed to a system of repayment that *de facto* meant that few Polish victims received adequate compensation in any way comparable to the victims in Western Europe or Soviet Union itself. Most of the Polish share of reparations was "given" to Poland by Soviet Union under the [Comecon](/wiki/Comecon "Comecon") framework, which was not only highly inefficient, but benefited Soviet Union much more than Poland. Under further Soviet pressure (related to the [London Agreement on German External Debts](/wiki/London_Agreement_on_German_External_Debts "London Agreement on German External Debts")), in 1953 the [People's Republic of Poland](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_Poland "People's Republic of Poland") renounced its right to further claims of reparations from the successor states of Nazi Germany. Only after the [fall of communism in Poland](/wiki/Fall_of_communism_in_Poland "Fall of communism in Poland") in 1989/1990 did the Polish government try to renegotiate the issue of reparations, but found little support in this from the German side and none from the Soviet (later, Russian) side.[[35]](#cite_note-Dingell-36)

The total number of forced labourers under Nazi rule who were still alive as of August 1999 was 2.3 million.[[1]](#cite_note-BeyerSchneider-1) The [German Forced Labour Compensation Programme](/wiki/German_Forced_Labour_Compensation_Programme "German Forced Labour Compensation Programme") was established in 2000; a forced labour fund paid out more than €4.37 billion to close to 1.7 million of then-living victims around the world (one-off payments of between €2,500 and €7,500).[[36]](#cite_note-Reuters-37) German Chancellor [Angela Merkel](/wiki/Angela_Merkel "Angela Merkel") stated in 2007 that "Many former forced labourers have finally received the promised humanitarian aid"; she also conceded that before the fund was established nothing had gone directly to the forced labourers.[[36]](#cite_note-Reuters-37) German president [Horst Koehler](/wiki/Horst_Koehler "Horst Koehler") stated:

> It was an initiative that was urgently needed along the journey to peace and reconciliation... At least, with these symbolic payments, the suffering of the victims has been publicly acknowledged after decades of being forgotten.[[36]](#cite_note-Reuters-37)
